comments: The numeric question for years as a researcher seems weird to me when there is no other identifying question for type of researcher. Also, the open access costs are unbelievable. It’s virtually never worth that money. Also, Neil degrasse Tyson famously said “in science when human behavior enters the equation things go nonlinear. That’s why physics is easy and sociology is hard.” The mores of science method as written are good for cultivating knowledge but largely unsuited for many of the questions I’m trying to answer. What’s more is that they arent practiced as written or credited.  And it’s really annoying putting up with hard-scientists who have not truly considered this problem talk about themselves as the only ones making new knowledge. Even if I conducted studies such that these data availability tools and registration were done, the hard-science interlocutor is not going to take it seriously as the cutting edge of our understanding on a topic. It’s a bar for gatekeeping a club for dweebs and not worth my time.

As someone who thinks a lot about science and scientific communication, I could not provide quality answer on many of the questions, because the methodologies and explained variances of explaining phenomena was just too encompassing.  I'm curious of your findings, as I imagine you will get findings more about the fields of science and disciplinary standards of ontology and epistemology.  I also think many of my answers reflect "current" structural dynamics of the discipline I practice, not the ideal of what scientific practice in my discipline 'should be'. gender_3_TEXT: As a devoted scientist, I'm not interested in gender theory.  You shouldn't be either.
method: Quasiexperimental studies with either secondary data or EMA data; Several clinical trails involving varying degrees of methodological rigor.
comments:
I 'strongly disagreed' that women and minorities are "just as welcome as..." men and White individuals in research environments, but the phrasing of the question likely subverts my meaning. In most university settings, the presence of White men is widely viewed as the problem to be solved.  I'm soundly middle aged.  The last time more men than women were enrolled in college was prior to my birth.  Those women, constituting the majority of their graduating classes, are now retirement aged.  The institutional bias against White men, particularly straight men, is explicit and unignorable.

The social sciences DOES have a very bad replication problem.  Several times I've unsuccessfully tried to replicate colleagues' findings with superior (larger N, better measurement, etc.).  Those studies are rarely published as result of a type-1 error.  To the contrary, they typically involve convoluted (moderated mediation, odd exclusionary criteria resulting in poor external validity, etc.), underpowered analyses, often with ill-suited data (often cross-sectional).  Reviewers should catch these things.  I believe the proliferation of open source journals has created pressure to overlook such methodological shortcomings.  A second source of pressure that is maliciously guiding the research environment is the translation of the neoliberal agenda (call it 'woke' if you like) through funding and editorial decisions, including the mob rule effect that has resulted in the withdraw of defensible empirical studies contradicting that neoliberal orthodoxy.

comments: Scientific knowledge valued by academic journals and universities, and academia more generally, is but one of virtually unlimited arenas or circuits of knowledge production and consumption. Objectivity/Subjectivity, Qualitative/Quantitative, and Disciplinary divides serve no real purpose than to further distinguish "scientifc knoweldge" from other epistemoplogy, and in so doing it forges a sense of legitimacy for "scientists" in the Western world who are encoruaged to pursue their intellectual interests only via the mean of legitimzied and institionalized practices and approaches. prereg_b_whynot_14_TEXT: Toward the end of my PhD, pre-registering studies was becoming more prevalent, but it seemed like such a time-consuming, clunky process. For novel research, you don't necessarily know what you are going to find, and pre-registering more final and concrete than simply forming hypotheses. Your hypothesis needed to be true.
OA_b_whynot_12_TEXT: It's not that journal policy prevented it; it just may not have been an option. Or, perhaps it cost additional money?
replication_b_whynot: I didn’t perceive my field to be in favor of this,I won’t be rewarded for it
replication_b_whynot_13_TEXT: In social and personality psychology, priority and privilege is allocated to novel research. I think there is now more openness to replication research, but I don't think it helps a person early in their career secure a position or get tenure. However, replication of a basic finding within a collection of studies published in a single paper (or perhaps a series of papers) is important. However, simply engaging in replication research of some one else's research doesn't seem as valuable in social and personality psychology.
comments: There are likely big disciplinary differences in attitudes toward "open science." Some disciplines reward and value replication research; other disciplines place a very strong emphasis on basic, novel research. Maybe things are different now, but 10 years ago, replication research wasn't going to get you a faculty appointment--at least not in social and personality psychology.

comments: There is a gap in research methods lately. When you are in grad/doctoral studies, no one teaches you how to conduct research anymore; it is expected that you already have all the necessary skills and ground knowledge. However, even knowing the skills the application is different, and no professor so far has taken the time to carefully explain how to write academically, how to write a grant proposal, how to analyze data appropriately. It's mostly about how much you are producing and how many conferences you are attending, rather than the quality of the work. As a first-generation and international student, much of my work is independent, and I struggle to get it done because I do not have the support to do so.

comments: I've seen more conversation about these practices (pre-registration and data sharing) for quantitative projects and data. I've heard less about them for qualitative projects and data. When you conduct qualitative research with marginalized populations (especially those openly targeted and maligned by the US government), sharing the data or even getting the project on people's radar beforehand feels like it could not only jeopardize the research but possibly endanger the participants. Replication also feels less relevant to qual researchers who do not aspire to objectivity and recognize that who we are as people shapes the research we produce (so naturally, someone else would "find" something different).

prereg_b_hesitate_14_TEXT: Look, whatever you preregister you're going to end up doing 'exploratory analyses' until you find something. The idea that you'll check off your last preregistered thing and say "oh well, I guess that was a waste of time and money" just isn't realistic. It's a nice idea, but agian, who is checking these? Maybe it reminds you to be careful and responsible with your stats, but again, we will keep going until we find something publishable because not doing so just isn't an option
OA_b_whynot: I didn’t think it was important,Other:
OA_b_whynot_12_TEXT: Open access has a lot of lofty goals that seem to be held mainly by older researchers. I mean, either the school pays journal subscription fees or the publisher pays open access fees. And like, who cares if it's open access? The research isn't necessarily better because no one is actually checking it. Reviewers are incentivized to nitpick either way, but even that doesn't guarantee fidelity of results. Ultimately it's about prestige or hiding your small effect sizes behind methods, I mean, is open access supposed to fix anything? It's fine. I'm not against it but I'm mainly skeptical that it changes anything about science
replication_b_whynot: I didn’t have sufficient resources available,It didn’t seem relevant for my work
comments: Interesting study! I don't think of myself as down on open science or anything. I'm just skeptical that these practices actually prevent anyone from being biased in their work. If anything I'd be in favor of doing only preprints. As I said, in my experience reviewers are incentivized to find fault with studies, and it kind of just gets in the way. The idea of preregistering is nice but again, we're not going to just give up on a project because we exhausted our preregistered analyses. And open data is fine, but unless it's a carefully curated data set meant for people to use, it's almost always going to be too difficult to be worth it.

This is heavily biased against people who use pre-existing data since it would be unlikely (or at least no one would believe) the data were not analyzed prior to registering. comments: People fail to make the distinction between values internal to science and values external to science.  Values internal to science, such as decisions about what theory to use, what method to use, how we measure our variables, etc. are relatively value free in the sense that these should be made known to the audience and can be debated if they are the best or not.  Values external to science are the moralistic values that we adopt such as promoting a given political cause.  These are deeply affected by our identities and position within the social structure and cannot be separated from those.  These should be kept out of the scientific discussion as they are unscientific.  But, just because I hold progressive values and want to promote equality, that does not mean I cannot do science objectively if I am transparent about what values I have used internal to science and do not publicize my values external to science.

comments: Whether or not cultural anthropology is a science is controversial. It is generally accepted within the field that ethnographic studies are functionally un-replicable on account of the fact that the researcher is also the basic data-gathering instrument, adding irreducible personality factors to the research. Moreover the cultural situations being researched change over historical time, including in response to the actions of previous ethnographers. Occasionally, anthropologists have gone to the same places and returned with different results; these are generally understood as producing more information about the same 'cultural situation' rather than refuting prior work, or evidence that the 'cultural situation' has changed over time. Most anthropologists would not regard these 'returns' as replication studies. Although cultural anthropologists do have theories that they take with them when they go out to do field research, these rarely take the form of testable hypotheses about which the research will return a true/false evaluation. Therefore preregistration is generally regarded as irrelevant to anthropologists. Data sharing among cultural anthropologists is complicated and controversial; since anthropologists are their own research instrument, data (fieldnotes) tend to take memoiristic forms that would do little to aid other researchers in the same field while creating ethical issues re. the identification of research subjects. This is especially the case where research subjects are involved in politically, legally, or ethically sensitive activities, which is not uncommon. However, there have been modest movements for anthropologists to share more of their fieldnotes.

comments: Some nuances that affected my answers but not be quantified: (1) Data sharing regarding qualitative data in my discipline (political science) is extremely complicated, as simple anonymity is often not sufficient for ethical sharing. The ethically correct amount of qualitative data to share can validly be "at the researcher's discretion and at the reviewer's tolerance." (2) While I agree that pre-registration is desirable for experimental work -- both for science as science and science as a profession, I would argue that it is difficult to articulate a case for expanding pre-registration to other realms in my discipline. By design, graduate students in social sciences are asked to shoulder the vast majority of the administrative burden of their research independently. Today, this already constitutes an unacceptable and extremely harmful waste of time. Any changes proposed in the name of "transparency" and "replicability" should be weighed against the uneven effects, across both resource levels and generational cohorts. (3) Open-access, in its current structural form, is a welfare program for rentier corporations with some of the highest profit margins in the world. Unwritten directly and indirectly with a combination of public funds and attrition on researcher time and resources.

replication_b_whynot_13_TEXT: I view the reality from the qualitative ontological perspective--meaning that the reality or meaning comes into being through interpretation and communication. So the knowledge is not out there in the 3 dimensional world but it is in our mind and we create it as we interpret the events. Like watching a movie the second or third time and each time you get a different meaning because time passed and you evolved so you look to the same event differently. Got it? Reality is never fixed and always changing. This is not a positivist approach but more like post-structuralist --and seems like more meaningful to me. So when it comes to replicating things-- the answers cannot be the same. It is the problem that Kuhn and Popper wanted to solve and none of them have achieved that. Don't try on this one is more recommendations.

prereg_b_hesitate_14_TEXT: It gets in the way of the scientific process, particularly in cases where replication is cheap. One often cannot anticipate what one will learn from data and if what one learns begs a question that was not part of the pre-registration, pre-registration can be a problem. It can get in the way of true discovery. And the problem is that a lot of people instead of thinking whether for a specific project pre-registration makes sense (and it often does), it is a rule that is applied in an absolute manner. And in my experience, people who cut corners without pre-registration keep on cutting corners with it.

replication_b_whynot_13_TEXT: Replication in a qualitative sense is much less clean cut. Even if I had access to the same data, if I was not present when it was being collected, my knowledge as an analyst would be reduced (not having the context etc) and so my analysis would not really be a replication. In the more qualitative and design studies I do, I often articulate what is similar and what is distinct about my work comapred to prior work, both in the related work and the discussion. So the new insights in my paper might say "Similar to XYZ, we also found ... " while "Distinct from ABC, we found ... " so in this way it is kind of a qualitative way of saying when we found something similar or different from past work. I think having another person go in and, for instance, qualitatively analysis interview transcripts when the interview was conducted by someone else, it could be interesting, but I think it would need to involve a conversation with someone who conducted the interviews or who understood more of the context surrounding the interviews, because it is very easy to misinterpret what people say out of context.

method: Literature review, comparing archeological sites, faunal analysis, osteological and paleopathological examination of skeletal remains, statistical analysis for skeletal variability and morphological variation of specific skeletal structures
comments: I am an archeologist which is often not considered a scientist, but I consider myself a scientist. Due to the nature of archeology I understand I can never know the full “truth” or all of the facts. For us this is because the archeological record is imperfect and incomplete, so we are always working from only a small part of a dataset. I am also an anthropologist first, and so acknowledge that truth is hard to define and often contextual, dependent on cultural norms and historical circumstance. I am in full support of data sharing in my field, but there are also unique barriers. For example, keeping Indigenous North American archeological sites safe often requires keeping the exact locations a secret and not publishing data sets which reference their coordinates. I think open science is incredibly important but we also have to assume that some people who access that information may not have the best intentions. It will be important to protect vulnerable or marginalized communities in the ways we share data and scientific findings and make sure the communities the research affects have a say in how and where the findings are made known.

comments: Once again speaking as an anthropologist, I was not sure how to describe the proportions of researcher attitudes precisely because there are such divergent perspectives. I do agree that our personal positionality does influence our research interests and likely influences how we interpret our results. However I do not think this means that there is no worth in pursuing objectivity, because in the attempt to be objective we can still identify and work to mitigate potential biases. That being said, I am more skeptical of those who have a financial interest (e.g., pharmaceutical research funded in whole or part by a particular drug manufacturer). In anthropology we curate and maintain community relationships which in my opinion *should* influence our work to be more in line with what interests or concerns the community in question has, rather than the researcher imposing their own "objective" viewpoint on the work. So I'm not sure how to personally square that, in that I am comfortable with self-reporting biases or relationships towards community members, which would not set off alarm bells as I read the research, versus financial backing, which in my opinion demonstrably reduces the reliability of research results.

comments: Though I enjoy curiosity-driven research projects and using both qualitative and quantitative approaches to develop and test social science models, I have become somewhat jaded about publishing. The ease of AI summaries means fewer students (and researchers) read and engage with academic articles. Further, when I review, I've noticed increased careless use of AI in generating text within submitted articles. I have less motivation to spend 200+ hours on a manuscript to prepare it for a top journal. I took a teaching position that allows me to creative research freedom while teaching and mentoring students. I can research what and when I want with little pressure from my department. I had a conversation with another faculty over whether we should conduct a larger series of studies (including replication) and write a book rather than publish in articles. We concluded few would likely read that either. I research more for personal enjoyment/learning now than to publish.
